metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete default timeout value (e.g., `10 seconds`) in the Security section and add a construction-time guard AC that verifies the httpx client is configured with that timeout (observable oracle: `client.timeout == 10`)
findings.finding: The Security section requires a 'bounded timeout' on the introspection HTTP call but states no concrete value or range; AC4 tests that a timeout causes a raise but never specifies the timeout duration to configure or assert, making the 'bounded' property unmeasurable and leaving an executor free to pick an arbitrary value.
findings.location: Security section ('bounded timeout'); AC4 ('non-200 / timeout / malformed response raises')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section replace 'bounded timeout' with 'a 10-second total timeout (connect + read)'; add a constructor parameter `timeout_seconds: float = 10.0` to `McpConfig` or the verifier constructor, and extend AC5 with: 'and that the httpx client is constructed with `timeout=10.0` (observable oracle: `client.timeout.read == 10.0`)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC6's integration test design: either name the FastMCP ASGI entrypoint (e.g. `srv.get_asgi_app()` or similar) if one exists, or pivot to the project's established pattern of `asyncio.run(srv.call_tool(...))` with an injected fake HTTP client on the verifier itself.
findings.finding: AC6 specifies an integration test using 'httpx `ASGITransport` wrapping the MCP server', but `build_server()` returns a `FastMCP` (stdio-transport) instance — no ASGI interface is established anywhere in the codebase, and ALL existing integration tests invoke the server by calling `srv.call_tool()` / `srv.list_tools()` directly, never via HTTP/ASGI. If `FastMCP` does not expose an ASGI app, the integration test as written cannot be wired up; the plan doesn't explain how to obtain the ASGI callable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC6 (integration test) / src/rebar/mcp_server.py build_server()

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `httpx` to the `[mcp]` optional-dependency group in `pyproject.toml` (or confirm that `mcp>=1.9` already pulls httpx transitively and document that as the dependency rationale), and verify the `[mcp]`-only optionality CI job still passes.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'reuse the already-present httpx' to justify using httpx directly in `_mcp_auth.py`, but httpx is NOT a declared dependency of the `[mcp]` extra (`pyproject.toml` line 82: `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9"]`). The codebase's optionality CI (`test_core_optionality.py`) explicitly classifies `httpx` as a heavy dep that arrives only via `[agents]`/pydantic-ai and must stay lazy everywhere. A user who installs `nava-rebar[mcp]` without `[agents]` will get an `ImportError` at runtime when the introspection verifier imports httpx.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — 'Reuse the already-present httpx'; pyproject.toml [project.optional-dependencies] mcp
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"httpx>=0.27"` to the `mcp` extra in pyproject.toml: `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9", "httpx>=0.27"]`. Alternatively, if mcp>=1.9 already requires httpx as a transitive dep, add a comment in pyproject.toml and the plan naming that transitive chain explicitly so it cannot silently break on an mcp version bump.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a concrete default timeout value (e.g., 5 s) in the Security section and add an AC oracle asserting the verifier raises within that bound when the fake AS is unresponsive.
findings.finding: The plan says 'bounded timeout' but never specifies the concrete timeout value, unit, or an AC done-definition. An implementer could set 30 s and still satisfy the spec, blocking the hot path for every token validation.
findings.location: Security section — 'bounded timeout'; AC has no corresponding timeout oracle
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Security: 'Default timeout: 5 s (configurable via auth_introspection_timeout_ms); raises TimeoutError after that bound.' Add an AC: 'Unit test asserts the verifier raises within the configured timeout when the fake AS stalls.'

findings.finding: The plan states 'bounded timeout' for the introspection HTTP call but never specifies the timeout value, its default, or whether it is a configurable key — so operators cannot tune it and no AC verifies it.
findings.location: Security section ('bounded timeout'); Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers timeout value or config)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `auth_introspection_timeout` (int, default 5) to the config key list alongside the other `auth_introspection_*` keys, and add an AC line: 'Unit test asserts the httpx introspection request is constructed with a timeout equal to `auth_introspection_timeout`; a fake that never responds triggers the timeout and raises within that bound.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that a missing/empty env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` must raise at construction time (not call time), and add a corresponding AC assertion: 'construction raises `ConfigurationError` when the named env var is absent' (observable oracle: `pytest.raises(ConfigurationError)`).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the behavior when the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is absent or empty at construction time, and no AC tests this guard — yet the security requirement ('no plaintext secret in config') implies a hard failure is required.
findings.location: What section ('auth_introspection_client_secret_env (NAME of an env var holding the client secret — no plaintext secret in config)'); AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC5: 'and that constructing the verifier when the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is unset raises `ConfigurationError` at construction time (observable oracle: `pytest.raises(ConfigurationError)`).'; add one sentence to the Security section: 'If the env var is absent or empty at construction time, raise `ConfigurationError` immediately — never defer to call time.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Security section and AC5 whether the private-host guard applies only to literal IP addresses in the URL (with a documented known gap for resolvable hostnames) or whether a DNS pre-resolution check at construction is required; if the latter, specify the mechanism (e.g. `socket.getaddrinfo` at construction time, or a connect-time `httpx` event hook) so the AC oracle covers both the IP-literal and hostname cases.
findings.finding: The construction-time private-host rejection mechanism only works for literal IP addresses in the endpoint URL; a hostname (e.g. `internal-auth.corp.local`) that resolves to an RFC 1918 or loopback address at DNS lookup time will pass the construction guard silently, making the SSRF protection ineffective for the most common real-world internal AS deployment pattern.
findings.location: Security section — 'Private/link-local endpoint hosts … are rejected by default'; AC5 — 'a private/link-local host is rejected … (observable oracle: rejection at construction for the http/private cases)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Security section: 'The construction-time guard checks the literal host component against private/loopback/link-local IP ranges using `ipaddress`; a hostname that resolves to a private IP is **not** caught at construction (documented known gap). Operators relying on `allow_private_host=false` should configure the endpoint as a literal-IP URL or enforce network-level controls.' Update AC5 to explicitly scope the oracle to literal-IP rejection so the implementation is not silently incomplete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the private-host guard specification to cover DNS-resolved addresses: either require a connect-time IP check (e.g. via httpx event hooks or a custom transport that resolves then validates the target IP before sending), or explicitly document that the guard covers literal IPs only and require operators to rely on network-layer controls for hostname-based private endpoints — and update AC5 to test a hostname that resolves to a private IP.
findings.finding: The private-host SSRF guard is specified as 'rejected at construction' and the AC5 oracle is 'rejection at construction for the http/private cases,' but Python's ipaddress module (the natural implementation tool) can only classify literal IP strings — a hostname like `internal-as.corp.local` that resolves to 10.x.x.x at connect time bypasses the construction-time guard silently, violating the stated security invariant that 'private/link-local endpoint hosts are rejected by default.'
findings.location: Security section — 'Private/link-local endpoint hosts (RFC 1918, loopback, 169.254.0.0/16) are rejected by default'; AC5 — 'a private/link-local host is rejected by default … observable oracle: rejection at construction for the http/private cases'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the Security section: 'The construction-time private-host check validates literal IP addresses in the URL; for hostname endpoints, a custom httpx transport (or event hook) that resolves the target IP and checks it against the RFC-1918/loopback/link-local ranges before sending is required to close the SSRF path for the hostname case.' Extend AC5: 'and that a hostname resolving to a private IP is also rejected (or document the limitation explicitly and note reliance on network-layer controls).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Security section (and add a corresponding AC) that a missing or empty resolved value for the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is a hard construction-time error (ConfigError / ValueError), so the verifier fails closed rather than proceeding with an empty credential.
findings.finding: The plan never specifies the fail-closed behavior when the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is absent or empty at runtime. If the verifier proceeds to make an introspection call with no credential (or an empty password), the `client_secret_basic` authentication guarantee on the boundary-crossing path is silently dropped — and if the AS accepts unauthenticated introspection, token validation becomes unauthenticated.
findings.location: Security section — 'client_secret_basic, secret from auth_introspection_client_secret_env'; What section — 'auth_introspection_client_secret_env (NAME of an env var holding the client secret)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section add: 'If the env var named by auth_introspection_client_secret_env is unset or empty at construction time, construction raises ConfigError — fail-closed.' Add an AC5 sub-case: 'and that constructing the verifier when the named env var is absent raises ConfigError (observable oracle: ConfigError at construction, never a verifier instance).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the failure protocol for an absent/empty `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` env var: add an AC or Security sub-bullet stating that construction raises `ConfigError` (fail-closed, fail-fast) when the named env var is not set or is empty.
findings.finding: The fallback behavior when `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` names an environment variable that is absent or empty at runtime is entirely unspecified. The plan defines the config key as holding the NAME of an env var (not the secret itself), but provides no decision rule for the secret-resolution failure path: should construction of `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` raise immediately? Should it raise at first call? What exception type? This is a structural gap in the sub-step failure protocol.
findings.location: What section — '`auth_introspection_client_secret_env` (NAME of an env var holding the client secret — no plaintext secret in config)'; Security section — 'secret sourced from `auth_introspection_client_secret_env`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'If the environment variable named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is absent or empty at construction time, raise `ConfigError` immediately (fail-closed, fail-fast — never send an empty credential).' Add a corresponding AC5 sub-bullet: 'construction raises when the named env var is missing.'

findings.finding: The Security section mandates that private/link-local endpoint hosts (RFC 1918, loopback, `169.254.0.0/16`) are 'rejected at construction' but never specifies the detection mechanism — whether the check is lexical (URL string / `urllib.parse` hostname extraction + `ipaddress` literal matching) or DNS-resolution-based (resolve the hostname and check the resulting IP). The two approaches have materially different security properties: a lexical check catches `192.168.1.1` but passes `internal-as.corp` (which resolves to a private IP), so a lexical-only implementation is bypassable by using a private hostname instead of a bare IP. Leaving the mechanism implementation-defined means the security control may not achieve its stated goal.
findings.location: Security section — first bullet: 'Private/link-local endpoint hosts … are rejected by default'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'The private-host check parses the literal IP address from the URL (via `ipaddress.ip_address(urllib.parse.urlparse(endpoint).hostname)`); hostnames are NOT DNS-resolved at construction time — an operator using a private hostname must set `allow_private_host=true` (documented risk).' OR: 'The check resolves the hostname at construction (via `socket.getaddrinfo`) and rejects any result that is_private/is_loopback/is_link_local.'

findings.finding: The plan specifies a 'bounded timeout' for the introspection HTTP call but declares no timeout value, no configurable field, and no default. An unspecified or excessively long timeout allows a slow or adversarial Authorization Server to hold open httpx connections indefinitely, creating a resource-exhaustion vector on the boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: Security section — 'bounded timeout'; Approach & rationale — 'Reuse the already-present httpx'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hard-coded default (e.g., `timeout=5.0`) to the httpx POST call, document it in the Security section, and extend AC3's 'non-200 / timeout / malformed response raises' oracle to include a slow-response scenario. Optionally expose it as `auth_introspection_timeout` (seconds) in the config list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `auth_introspection_timeout_seconds` (float, default e.g. 5.0) to the `auth_introspection_*` config key set, `McpConfig`, `_SECTIONS['mcp']`, and `MCP_ENV_VARS`, so the HTTP timeout is operator-configurable rather than hardcoded.
findings.finding: The introspection HTTP call timeout is described only as 'bounded timeout' with no specified value and no config key exposed to operators. A hardcoded timeout that cannot be tuned is a maintainability gap: operators with slow AS endpoints or strict latency budgets must change code rather than config.
findings.location: Security section — 'bounded timeout'; Acceptance Criteria — no timeout config key listed among auth_introspection_* fields
findings.suggested_fix: Add `auth_introspection_timeout_seconds: float = 5.0` to McpConfig and the corresponding `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_INTROSPECTION_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` env var; pass it as the `timeout=` argument to the httpx call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete closed vocabulary for `mcp.auth_strategies` valid tokens (e.g., `{"introspection", "jwt", ...}`) in this plan (or add an explicit cross-reference to the S2 plan section that enumerates it), so the `_SECTIONS['mcp']` coercer can use `_as_choice`/`_as_str_tuple`+validation and reject unknown tokens at load time.
findings.finding: The `mcp.auth_strategies` config key is referenced as the activation seam for `IntrospectionTokenVerifier`, but its complete valid-value vocabulary (the full enum of recognized strategy tokens) is never defined in this plan. The plan says S2 'introduced' this key but provides no cross-reference to where S2 enumerates valid tokens. An implementer writing the `_SECTIONS['mcp']` coercer for `auth_strategies` cannot validate or reject tokens (e.g., 'introspect' vs 'introspection') without a canonical vocabulary list.
findings.location: What section — 'activated by including `introspection` in the `mcp.auth_strategies` list (the strategy-selection config key **introduced by S2**)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the What or Scope section: 'The full valid vocabulary for `auth_strategies` is `{"introspection"}` (this story) plus any tokens S2/S3/S5/S6 add; the coercer validates each element against this set and raises `ConfigError` on an unknown token.' Then add an AC to verify that an unknown strategy token raises `ConfigError`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observable oracle to AC #2 (or a new AC) asserting the client_id fallback chain: a response with `client_id` present maps correctly; absent `client_id` + `sub` present → `client_id==sub`; both absent → `client_id=="unknown"`.
findings.finding: The `client_id` response-field mapping described in the What section ('client_id→client_id (absent → falls back to sub, else "unknown")') has no covering acceptance criterion — no AC tests that this fallback chain is implemented correctly.
findings.location: What section (client_id mapping) vs. Acceptance Criteria (all ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC #2 'active:true' happy-path assertion to include `client_id` in the expected AccessToken fields, and add two distinct negative controls (absent client_id with sub present; both absent) either in AC #2's list or as a new bullet in AC #3.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a requirement (Security or AC) that transport errors (non-200, timeout, malformed) are logged at ERROR level with the status code/error type but WITHOUT the token or secret, and define whether a counter metric is emitted.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability for introspection call failures — no structured log at error level, no metric, and no alert. Operators have no signal when the AS is down or consistently rejecting tokens.
findings.location: Security section; Acceptance Criteria section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Security: 'On transport failure, log at ERROR: {endpoint, http_status_or_error_type} — never include token or secret. Increment a rebar_mcp_introspection_error_total counter if metrics are enabled.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly acknowledge the no-retry / no-circuit-breaker tradeoff in the plan (security rationale vs. availability), and add an AC or operator note specifying what operators should do when the AS is unreliable (e.g., configure a health check or accept the availability dependency).
findings.finding: The plan's fail-closed design (no retry, no circuit-breaker) means a single transient AS hiccup instantly denies all token validations with no degraded mode. The availability tradeoff is unacknowledged and no circuit-breaker or operator guidance is provided.
findings.location: Security section — 'Fail-closed transport'; 'No caching in v1'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to Security: 'No retry in v1 (retrying could mask revocation); operators who need resilience against transient AS failures should place a reverse proxy or load balancer in front of the AS. A circuit-breaker is a documented follow-on.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly add `httpx` to the `mcp` optional-dependency group in `pyproject.toml` (e.g. `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9", "httpx>=0.27"]`) so the introspection verifier's HTTP client is a declared, version-bounded dependency rather than an implicit transitive one.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'reuse the already-present httpx' as a rationale for using httpx directly in the new verifier, but httpx is NOT a declared dependency of the `mcp` extra (pyproject.toml line 82: `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9"]`). It is only available transitively through mcp>=1.9 and pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic]. Relying on a transitive dependency for direct, first-class HTTP calls in production auth code is fragile — a future mcp or pydantic-ai release that drops or replaces its httpx dependency would silently break the verifier at runtime without any declared-dep violation.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section / pyproject.toml line 82
findings.suggested_fix: In pyproject.toml, update line 82 to: `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9", "httpx>=0.27"]`. Also add `httpx>=0.27` to the `dev` extra if not already transitively guaranteed there, to keep local and CI parity. Document the floor version as the one that introduced `httpx.BasicAuth` (available since httpx 0.18).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to the Security section (and a corresponding AC5 or implementation note) specifying that the httpx client used for introspection calls MUST NOT log Authorization headers — either by constructing the client without attaching debug event hooks, or by explicitly filtering the Authorization header from any log output — and verify this in a unit test or code-review checklist entry.
findings.finding: The plan declares 'Tokens/secrets never logged' but provides no AC or implementation note to prevent the httpx client from emitting the `Authorization` header (which carries the Base64-encoded `client_secret_basic` credential) at DEBUG level via httpx's built-in request/response event hook logging — a standard httpx integration pattern that operators and frameworks routinely enable.
findings.location: Security section — 'Tokens/secrets never logged'; AC5 — no assertion about header logging suppression
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section add: 'The httpx client must be constructed without event hooks that emit Authorization headers; if a custom transport or logging hook is used, the Authorization header must be redacted before logging.' Add an AC note: 'Implementation note: do not attach httpx event_hooks that log request headers; verify by code review that no AUTH_HEADER_NAME appears in any log call path.'

findings.finding: The What section describes two specific AccessToken field mappings — `sub → subject (absent → None)` and `client_id → (falls back to sub, else 'unknown')` — but no acceptance criterion contains an observable oracle verifying these mappings. AC2 only asserts 'expected scopes, resource==auth_resource_server_url, and a positive expires_at', leaving the subject and client_id fallback logic as uncovered described work.
findings.location: What section (AccessToken field mappings); AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2 to read: '…expected scopes, `resource==auth_resource_server_url`, a positive `expires_at`, `subject==<expected sub>`, and `client_id==<expected client_id>`; and that when the response omits `client_id` but includes `sub`, `AccessToken.client_id == sub`; and when both are absent, `AccessToken.client_id == "unknown"`.

findings.finding: The Security section explicitly requires 'Never emit expires_at=0', yet no AC verifies the specific boundary where the AS returns exp=0 (a technically valid but semantically degenerate introspection response). Without a test, an implementation that maps exp=0 directly to AccessToken.expires_at=0 satisfies all current ACs while violating the stated security requirement.
findings.location: Security section ('Never emit expires_at=0') vs. AC2 (only tests exp present → positive value and exp absent → None)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'and that when the AS returns exp=0, expires_at is set to None (observable oracle: expires_at is None, not 0), because exp=0 is semantically equivalent to absent/invalid and the SDK reads a falsy expires_at as no-expiry.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR entry or `CLAUDE.md` / design-doc section capturing (a) why a custom verifier is written instead of importing the SDK class, (b) the fail-closed transport-error policy, and (c) the no-cache-in-v1 decision with the revocation-delay tradeoff, so this rationale is discoverable in the repo without reading the ticket.
findings.finding: Three significant architectural decisions — (1) writing a custom verifier rather than importing the SDK class, (2) fail-closed transport errors, (3) deliberately no caching in v1 — are discussed inline but the plan names no ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update to capture this rationale durably. Future maintainers who didn't read this ticket have no discoverable record of why these choices were made.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section; Security section ('No caching in v1'); What section ('NOTE: the SDK's IntrospectionTokenVerifier is an example…')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Approach section that the verifier holds a single shared httpx.AsyncClient (or httpx.Client for sync), created at construction and closed at server shutdown, and add an AC asserting it is not re-created per request.
findings.finding: The lifecycle of the httpx client inside IntrospectionTokenVerifier is unspecified — the plan doesn't say whether the client is created once (shared across concurrent requests) or per-call, leaving resource efficiency and concurrency safety undefined.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — 'Reuse the already-present httpx'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach: 'The verifier creates one httpx.AsyncClient at construction time (async context) and reuses it across calls; the MCP server's lifespan closes it. Never create a client per introspection call.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py` is created by S2 (making it an explicit S2 deliverable) or by S4 (update plan wording from 'add to' to 'create'); the file does not currently exist.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py` does not exist in the codebase; the plan says S4 adds `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` 'to `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py`', implying the file pre-exists. The file must be created by S4 (or by a dependency), but the plan's phrasing treats it as an existing module.
findings.location: What section: 'Add an IntrospectionTokenVerifier to src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py'
findings.suggested_fix: If S2 owns the file, add it to S2's deliverables. If S4 owns it, change 'Add an IntrospectionTokenVerifier to src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py' to 'Create src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py and add IntrospectionTokenVerifier to it'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the exact S2 deliverables (config key names, field types, `McpConfig` field names) this plan depends on and verify they match S2's plan/AC, since neither `auth_strategies` nor `auth_resource_server_url` currently exist in the codebase.
findings.finding: The plan's S2-seam assertions — `mcp.auth_strategies` config key and `auth_resource_server_url` — do not exist anywhere in the codebase. The plan treats these as 'introduced by S2, this story's dependency', but S2 has not shipped. If S4 is written to depend on these, and S2 delivers them differently (different field names, different locations), S4's implementation will be wrong.
findings.location: What section: 'activated by including `introspection` in the `mcp.auth_strategies` list (the strategy-selection config key introduced by S2)' and 'validates against the single `auth_resource_server_url` (S2)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that S1's scope explicitly covers fixing `gen_mcp_reference.py`'s hardcoded 3-var gate table (lines 208–209) to iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS`, and confirm the ordering dependency S1 → S4/S7 is explicit in the epic sequencing.
findings.finding: The plan states 'S1 fixes the generators to iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS`' as a scoping exclusion, but `gen_mcp_reference.py` currently hardcodes exactly 3 gate env-var names (lines 208–209: `REBAR_MCP_READONLY`, `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM`, `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`) rather than iterating `MCP_ENV_VARS`. New `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_INTROSPECTION_*` entries added to `MCP_ENV_VARS` by S4 will NOT appear in `docs/mcp-reference.md` until S1 ships. If S7 regenerates docs before S1 lands, the reference will be incomplete.
findings.location: What section: 'The generated mcp-reference.md/env-vars.md are regenerated for the full key set by S7; S1 fixes the generators to iterate MCP_ENV_VARS'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the MCP SDK `simple-auth` example with `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` exists in the installed SDK version (check the SDK's GitHub or package source), and pin the specific SDK version / file path the implementation should adapt from.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the SDK's `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` is an example, not an exported/hardened class' as the rationale for writing a custom one — but `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` is not importable from any `mcp.*` module path in the installed SDK. The 'example' claim cannot be verified from the installed package, meaning the 'simple-auth example' reference the plan relies on for adaptation may exist only in the SDK's source tree (not the installed package), or may not exist at all in this SDK version.
findings.location: What section: 'NOTE: the SDK's IntrospectionTokenVerifier is an example, not an exported/hardened class — this story WRITES its own (adapting the example), it does not import it.' / Approach section: 'Adapt the SDK simple-auth example (the blessed reference)'

findings.finding: The Security section's cache formula `min(now + auth_introspection_cache_ttl, token_exp)` is stated as the universal rule, but `token_exp` is `None` when the introspection response omits `exp` — which RFC 7662 explicitly permits and the plan explicitly allows. In Python, `min(int, None)` raises `TypeError`, so any implementation that applies the formula literally will crash on the first `active:true` response without an `exp` claim when caching is enabled. The Expiry sub-section alludes to the correct fallback (`now + cache_ttl`) but never gives the conditional formula that covers both branches, leaving an implementer reading only the Cache paragraph to write a runtime crash.
findings.location: Security section — Cache paragraph; Expiry sub-section
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the single formula with an explicit two-branch rule in the Cache paragraph: 'cache_deadline = (min(now + cache_ttl, token_exp) if token_exp is not None else now + cache_ttl)'.

findings.finding: The Security/Cache paragraph states the universal cache TTL formula as `min(now + auth_introspection_cache_ttl, token_exp)`, but the Expiry sub-section (physically separate) states that `token_exp` may be `None` when `exp` is absent. The processing decision rule for the `exp`-absent + caching-enabled combination is never stated in the cache formula section — only inferable by cross-reading two non-adjacent paragraphs. An implementer reading only the cache rule will write `min(int, None)` → `TypeError` at runtime on any valid `active:true` response that omits `exp` when caching is enabled.
findings.location: Security section — Cache paragraph (`min(now + auth_introspection_cache_ttl, token_exp)`); Expiry sub-section (`exp` absent → `expires_at=None`)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the cache formula sentence with: 'A positive is cached until `min(now + auth_introspection_cache_ttl, token_exp)` when `exp` is present; when `exp` is absent, cached until `now + auth_introspection_cache_ttl` (no token-expiry bound). Neither form caches errors or `active:false` responses.'

findings.finding: AC4 claims a positive is cached until `min(now + cache_ttl, token_exp)`, but no observable oracle or mechanism (e.g., an injectable clock/time dependency) is specified for verifying the cache-expiry boundary in-session. Without a stated time-injection seam, an executor cannot deterministically test that the cache entry expires at exactly the earlier of the two bounds — they can only test that it is present at time-of-creation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: cache expiry claim
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, append: 'Cache expiry is tested by passing a controllable `now` callable (or `time.time` override) into the verifier; the test asserts the entry is absent when simulated time exceeds `min(creation_time + cache_ttl, token_exp)` and present before that boundary.'

findings.finding: The cache TTL boundary condition — when `token_exp < now + cache_ttl` — has no specified observable oracle in AC4, meaning an implementation that caches until `now + cache_ttl` (ignoring `token_exp`) would pass all acceptance criteria while introducing a security bug (token cached past its own expiry).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (cache behavior)
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, add: 'and that when the token's own exp is sooner than now+cache_ttl, the cached entry expires at token_exp (observable oracle: advancing a mocked clock past token_exp causes a cache miss and a re-call to the fake, even when cache_ttl has not yet elapsed).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
